item.title: The Library
item.description:
Libraries have become a huge part of my life. They always have been.

People sometimes ask how I ended up becoming a librarian. Colleagues ask. Patrons ask. Friends ask.

I always tell them the same story.

When I was a kid, my best friends were my sister and Mouse. We’d disappear into the stacks together and do exactly the things you weren’t supposed to do. We’d sneak snacks, settle into a quiet corner, and disappear into stories.

I’ve always thought that was the perfect use of a library.

Libraries are strange places. They have a way of dissolving time and space until all that’s left is imagination, observation, and reflection—three things I seem to organize much of my life around.

Working in libraries hasn’t changed that belief. If anything, it’s strengthened it.

Part of the reason I share so openly is because I don’t think of myself as library police. I don’t create rules for how people should engage with imagination. I don’t decide what someone should wonder about.

I’m simply a host.

The library belongs to the people who walk through its doors. My job is to help them find what they’re looking for—or sometimes, the thing they didn’t know they needed until they stumbled across it.

Sometimes that thing is a book. Sometimes it’s a quiet place to think. Sometimes it’s just a place to sneak a snack.

My personal library is a little different.

Its shelves are filled according to curiosity rather than any system. Books. Comics. Old magazines. Things that might make your grandma clutch her rosary. Folklore. Anthropology. Architecture. Fantasy. Strange ideas. Beautiful ideas. Contradictory ideas.

I collect the things that make me stop and wonder about the world.

Someone once said that books let you live hundreds of lives.

I think that’s true.

Maybe that’s why I keep collecting them.

If you’ve wandered this far and something on my shelves catches your curiosity, you’re welcome to ask about a library card at the Front Desk. I’d be happy to share a few of the quieter shelves.

item.title: The Frame
item.description:
Aren’t We Lucky?

We spend our days sending each other little pieces of life.

A goofy selfie that made me miss your face. A margarita glowing against a San Antonio sunset that somehow turns into plans for SeaWorld. A picture of Figs and Loki doing something ridiculous. A random chicken sandwich that looked particularly excellent.

The conversation keeps moving, and those little moments are carried away by the current. Knowing myself, I probably won’t ever scroll back far enough to see them again.

So I bought a shared digital picture frame.

It sits just inside the threshold of my home, where I pass by it several times a day. Every time I walk past, it’s quietly showing me little glimpses into the lives of the people I care about.

I love it.

I can’t tell you how happy it makes me to see Figs and Loki, Jayden, Gary, Kobe, Theodore, and all the little moments that have already started collecting there. It still amazes me that someone can notice something, share it, and a few moments later it quietly becomes part of my day.

The best part is that the photos don’t have to be extraordinary.

In fact, I hope they aren’t.

Show me your day. Your pets. Your shopping trip. The hot dude or dudette you happened to notice while you were out. A rainy morning. A project you’re proud of. The weird thing you found at the thrift store. The random chicken sandwich that looked too good not to photograph. Something you took a picture of without really knowing why. Those are my favorite kinds of photos.

They’re traces.

One observer quietly saying to another,

“This made me stop.”

I’d love to see it too.

The Frame isn’t an archive.

I already have journals, photographs, and stories that preserve history. The Frame serves a different purpose. It lets the people I care about quietly continue showing up in my day. A little window into whatever they’ve been noticing lately.

Every photo reminds me that, for just a moment, you thought,

“Spyke might appreciate this.”

That simple thought carries more meaning than the photograph itself.

Because if it mattered enough to make you stop… …I’d love to stop there with you.

If you’re part of my life and would like to leave the occasional trace on the Frame, don’t be surprised when I send you an invitation.

I just really miss your face.

item.title: See_You_At_Sunrise
item.description:
Day 1

Inside out. Did major cutting on my shirt. Perfect! Perfect! Forgot what we ate day one. Oh! I think it was peanut butter oats with apple crisp topping at Winnie and Ethels Diner.

Day 2

Squirtle squad! Blowing bubble attacks on people was fun! Another sunrise and another lineup schedule that was awesome. I know I had something but I don’t remember what. Oh! A donut, fruity pebbles. It was loaded with frosting and rolled in cereal. I had a vanilla coffee with almond milk I think. We hunted collectibles, visiting all the stages. The car ride home was looooong.

Day 3

Freezing wind! I survived. Made it to the car. Lumpia and bulgogi.

item.title: Terminal Ritual
item.description: Small remote magic tonight. Is this digital scrying? Anyway here’s a photo of the sun from my window.

item.title: Sunday Morning
item.description:
Hydra engaged. Gave it a snack and then it wouldn’t stop following me. This is a terribly coded attempt at metaphor for I logged into Disney+ at home (subscription accidentally renewed, not mine, but might as well use it). Well… when in rome… I searched for Disney Classics and got Scrubs, Designing Women, Bewitched (a good one). Guess I’ll be keeping an eye out for DVDs. I just can’t with 20 tags for your whole library. Anyway where is filtering? Disney’s Junk Drawer.

Anyway, the demon and I posted a reel. But he’s bound to the Darkroom because he gets into everything and I’m bound to him. We’ll make the best of it.

item.title: First Sighting
item.description: Perched at the dais the beams find me. Dust motes come to my rescue flying between me and discovery. I’ve just got one more thing to do here.
